title: Use std::min from the algorithm header
prompt_md:
## Goal
Reimplement a tiny utility by looking up the correct standard-library function. Read two integers from input and print the smaller of them on one line. You must use the standard-library function whose cppreference page begins with the signature shown below — do not write the comparison yourself with `if`/`?:`.

```cpp
// from https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/algorithm/min (overload 1)
// template< class T >
// constexpr const T& min( const T& a, const T& b );
```

## Input
A single line containing two integers separated by a space.

## Output
One line containing the smaller of the two integers, followed by a newline.

## Requirements
1. Include the standard header that contains the function shown in the snippet (the cppreference page tells you which one).
2. Read two `int` values from `std::cin`.
3. Call the standard-library function from the snippet to compute the smaller value.
4. Print the result, followed by a newline.

## Notes
The function you need lives in the `<algorithm>` header. If the two inputs are equal, the function returns the first argument — printing either is fine for the test cases (they avoid equal values for non-tied cases and tie inputs produce that same value either way).

title: Fix the wrong standard-library call
prompt_md:
## Goal
The program below is supposed to read three integers and print the largest of them. It calls a standard-library function with the right syntax, but the wrong function name. Use the cppreference signatures below to identify the correct function and swap it in. The current code calls `std::min` (smallest), but the goal needs the function whose page begins:

```cpp
// from https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/algorithm/max
// constexpr T max( std::initializer_list<T> ilist );
```

## Input
A single line containing three integers separated by spaces.

## Output
One line containing the largest of the three integers, followed by a newline.

## Requirements
1. Keep the `#include` lines, variable declarations, and input reads as written.
2. Change the function name on the single call line so the program returns the *largest* value, not the smallest.
3. The braced-initializer-list syntax `{ a, b, c }` is already correct; only the function name needs fixing.
4. The program must compile cleanly under `g++ -std=c++20 -Wall -Wextra`.

## Notes
The planted bug is `std::min({ a, b, c })`. The fix is `std::max({ a, b, c })` — same overload pattern (initializer-list overload), opposite direction.
